title: Heatwave impact: Mysuru hospitals on alert, tourism records sharp dip - The Hindu
full_text:
Amid the searing summer heat taking a toll on people—particularly the elderly and children—a dedicated ward has been set up at K.R. Hospital and Cheluvamba Hospital, both attached to the Mysore Medical College and Research Institute (MMCRI), to treat heatstroke cases.
With rising temperatures and delayed pre-monsoon showers, heat stress is emerging as a growing concern. Anticipating an increase in such cases, the MMCRI has put special arrangements in place to treat patients affected by extreme heat exposure.
MMCRI dean and director, K.R. Dakshayani, told The Hindu that a 20-bed special ward has been established in the stone building of Krishna Rajendra Hospital, while separate 10-bed wards for women and children have been arranged at Cheluvamba Hospital.
“So far, no heatstroke cases have been reported at the hospitals, but all necessary precautions have been taken in view of the intense heat,” she said.
Patients arriving at emergency units with symptoms such as sunstroke, severe dehydration, or related conditions are being immediately referred to these special wards, where doctors are prepared to provide treatment in line with established medical guidelines.
Dr. Dakshayani advised people with comorbidities such as diabetes and hypertension to take extra precautions, as extreme heat can aggravate existing conditions. They were advised to stay hydrated by drinking plenty of water and to avoid unnecessary exposure to the sun during peak hours.
Children, pregnant women and the elderly are particularly vulnerable and should exercise caution. In severe cases, heatstroke can lead to collapse and loss of consciousness, making timely medical intervention critical, she added.
Further, the prolonged dry spell has made the summer increasingly unbearable. Even as swimming pools across the city report a surge in visitors seeking relief from the heat, juice centres and tender coconut stalls are also witnessing a steady rush, with heat-weary residents thronging these outlets for a brief respite with refreshing drinks.
Dip in footfall
Meanwhile, tourist footfall has dipped, with stakeholders blaming the intense heatwave gripping the region.
Mysuru Hotel Owners’ Association president C. Narayanagowda said, “This is typically the time when Mysuru attracts a large number of tourists. However, this year, the heatwave has impacted footfall. Hotels have recorded only about 50% occupancy. Tourists and restaurants, too, are witnessing a decline in business. This can be attributed to the prevailing heatwave conditions, which are not limited to Mysuru but are being experienced across the State.”
Published - April 22, 2026 07:14 pm IST
